title: The trigger of someone commenting on your body or appearance
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Someone said something about my body today 

 It was a simple comment. Someone I barely know looked at me and said, "You look tired." And I felt my chest...
htmlContent:
Someone said something about my body today

It was a simple comment. Someone I barely know looked at me and said, "You look tired." And I felt my chest tighten. My shoulders went up toward my ears. My jaw clenched. I smiled anyway, said something polite, and walked away. But inside, something had shifted. The rest of my day was colored by that one sentence. And I couldn't shake it.

Maybe for you it's different. Maybe someone said "You look great, have you lost weight?" and you felt a surge of panic instead of pleasure. Or maybe they said nothing at all, but you caught them looking. That split-second glance at your stomach, your arms, your thighs. And you knew. You knew what they were thinking. Or at least, your nervous system told you that you knew.

Here's the thing. Comments about our bodies and appearance are never just comments. They land on a nervous system that has been shaped by years, sometimes decades, of being watched, evaluated, judged. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote extensively about how trauma lives in the body, would say that these moments activate our oldest survival responses. Your body remembers. Before your conscious mind can even process the words, your nervous system has already decided: danger.

I want to talk about why this happens. Why a single comment can ruin your day, your week, your sense of safety in your own skin. And what we can do about it, without adding more shame or pressure to "fix" ourselves.

The body remembers what the mind tries to forget

Let me be clear about something. The problem is not that you are too sensitive. The problem is not that you need thicker skin. The problem is that your nervous system is doing exactly what it evolved to do: scanning for threat and responding accordingly.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades studying how animals in the wild process threat. A gazelle that escapes a lion doesn't spend the next week ruminating on the encounter. It shakes, it trembles, it discharges the survival energy, and then it goes back to grazing. But humans? We don't get to do that. We get told to "calm down" or "let it go" or "don't be so dramatic." So the energy stays. And the next time someone makes a comment, that stored energy gets activated all over again.

When someone comments on your body, it's not just about that moment. It's about every moment you were ever made to feel wrong in your body. Every time your mother made a comment about your weight. Every time a partner compared you to someone else. Every time a stranger yelled something from a car. Every time a doctor dismissed your pain because of your size or your gender or your age. Your body keeps the score, and that score includes every single one of those moments.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work explores how trauma can be passed down through generations, including the way our families taught us to relate to our bodies. It's possible that the sensitivity you feel around appearance comments didn't even start with you. It might have started with your grandmother, or your great-grandmother, and the messages got passed down like an heirloom nobody asked for.

Why the comment hits so hard

Let's get specific about what actually happens in your body when someone comments on your appearance. First, there's the freeze. A split second where time seems to stop. Then there's the flood. Heat in your face, tightness in your chest, a knot in your stomach. Maybe you feel dizzy or disconnected, like you're watching yourself from outside your body. That's dissociation. That's your nervous system saying "this is too much, we're leaving."

And then there's the aftermath. The replay. You go over the comment in your mind again and again. What did they mean by that? Was it a compliment? An insult? A neutral observation that you're reading too much into? You try to figure out whether you're allowed to be upset. You search for evidence that you're overreacting. You tell yourself to just get over it. But you can't.

Here's what's actually going on. Your nervous system is not trying to figure out whether the comment was "really" a threat. It's responding to the pattern. If you grew up in an environment where your appearance was constantly monitored, criticized, or used as a measure of your worth, then any comment about your body is a match for that old wound. It doesn't matter if the comment is positive or negative. The activation is the same.

Think about it. Someone says "You look amazing today." And instead of feeling good, you feel terrified. Because now there's a standard to maintain. Now you have to keep looking amazing. Now you have to worry about the day when you don't. That's not a compliment. That's a threat disguised as kindness. And your nervous system knows the difference.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma-informed treatment, talks about how the parts of us that hold these wounds don't have a sense of time. The part of you that was 12 years old and humiliated in gym class is still there. The part of you that was 16 and told you'd be prettier if you just lost a few pounds is still there. When someone makes a comment today, those parts come forward. They don't know it's 2024. They think it's happening all over again.

So no, you're not overreacting. You're having a perfectly reasonable response to an experience that your nervous system has learned to treat as dangerous. The goal is not to stop having that response. The goal is to help your nervous system learn, slowly and safely, that it doesn't have to respond that way anymore.

What actually helps when someone comments on your body

I'm not going to tell you to "just love yourself" or "don't let their words affect you." That's toxic positivity dressed up as advice, and it's insulting to the complexity of what you're experiencing. Your nervous system doesn't care about affirmations. It cares about safety. So let's talk about what actually creates safety.

First, pause. Do not try to respond immediately. You are allowed to take a breath. You are allowed to say nothing. You are allowed to change the subject. You are allowed to walk away. The priority is not politeness. The priority is regulating your nervous system. If that means being rude, be rude. Your safety matters more than their comfort.

Second, orient to the present moment. Look around the room. Find five things you can see. Three things you can hear. One thing you can feel, like your feet on the floor or the weight of your body in the chair. This is not a distraction. This is a direct intervention in your nervous system. Dan Siegel's work on "window of tolerance" shows that when we're outside that window, we need to bring ourselves back before we can think clearly. Orientation does that.

Third, notice what your body is doing. Are your shoulders up? Drop them. Is your jaw tight? Let it soften. Are you holding your breath? Exhale slowly. You don't need to fix everything. Just make one small adjustment. That's enough. That's a signal to your nervous system that you are here, you are present, and you are taking care of yourself.

Fourth, don't try to figure out what the comment "really meant." That's a trap. You'll never know for sure, and the rumination will keep you activated. Instead, focus on what you need right now. Do you need to cry? Do you need to move your body? Do you need to talk to someone who gets it? Do you need to be alone? Trust that. Your needs are valid.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help your nervous system shift out of threat mode and into a state of rest. I keep one on my couch for exactly these moments. When I feel the activation rising, I wrap myself in it and let the weight do its work. It's not a cure. But it's a tool. And tools matter.

Building a different relationship with your body over time

I want to be honest with you. This work is slow. You don't heal decades of body shame in a weekend retreat or a single therapy session. But you can start building a different relationship with your body, one small moment at a time.

One thing that helped me was learning to separate the comment from the story. The comment is just words. The story is everything your nervous system adds to those words. And you can start to notice the story without believing it. "Ah, there's the story about how I'm not good enough. There's the story about how my body is wrong. There's the story about how I need to fix myself before I can be safe." You don't have to fight the story. You just have to see it.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises that help you track your triggers, understand your responses, and build skills for regulation. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about understanding yourself. And that understanding is the foundation for real change.

Another thing that helps is building a practice of noticing what your body does for you, rather than what it looks like. This sounds cheesy, I know. But stay with me here. Your legs carried you to the bathroom this morning. Your hands made your coffee. Your lungs breathed in and out without you having to think about it. Your body is not an object to be evaluated. It is the vehicle of your life. And when you start to relate to it that way, the comments from other people start to lose some of their power.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk's work has helped millions of people understand why their bodies react the way they do. It's not an easy read, but it's an important one. It can help you make sense of your experience, and that sense-making is itself a form of healing.

The comments will keep coming. But you can change how you meet them.

I wish I could tell you that people will stop commenting on your body. They won't. We live in a world that treats bodies as public property, as objects to be evaluated and judged. That's not going to change overnight. But what can change is your relationship to those comments. Not by building a wall. Not by becoming numb. But by learning to meet them with more compassion for yourself and more understanding of what's actually happening in your nervous system.

The next time someone says something about your body, try this. Pause. Breathe. Notice what your nervous system is doing. And then say to yourself, quietly, "This is hard. And I am handling it." Because you are. You're reading this article. You're doing the work. You're yourself in the only way that matters. Not perfectly. Not without pain. But with willingness. And that is enough.

  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
